title: Advancing the Path to Success: How States Can Teach the Success Sequence to Youth | Institute for Family Studies
gist: Alan J. Hawkins and Connie Huber make the empirical and policy case for states teaching the 'success sequence' (finish high school, work full-time, marry before children) to youth, arguing it nearly eliminates poverty risk, answering its main critics, and laying out a federalist, standards-based implementation plan funded via TANF and marriage-license fees.

insights.claim: Young adults who complete high school, work full-time, and marry before having children — in that order — have a 97% chance of avoiding poverty in their mid-30s and 86% reach at least the middle class.
insights.speaker: Alan J. Hawkins, Connie Huber
insights.so_what: The headline statistic underpinning a growing red/blue-state policy push toward family-sequencing curricula.
insights.quote: 97% of them (and any kids they may have) are not in poverty in their mid-30s, and 86% reach at least the middle class

insights.claim: The benefits are robust across demographics: only 4% of Black and 3% of Hispanic Millennials who follow the sequence are poor, and only 6% of those from lower-income backgrounds and 5% of those raised without both biological parents are poor as adults if they follow it.
insights.speaker: Alan J. Hawkins, Connie Huber
insights.so_what: Cross-demographic robustness is the authors' key rebuttal to the 'privilege/no-real-choice' critique.
insights.quote: only 4% of Black Millennials and 3% of Hispanic Millennials who follow the success sequence are poor
insights.locator: And it’s not just economic success

insights.claim: The odds of being poor approaching midlife are more than 10 times greater for young adults who do not follow the sequence, and following it cuts the odds of poor emotional health in 30-somethings in half even after controls.
insights.speaker: Alan J. Hawkins, Connie Huber
insights.so_what: Extends the case from economics to mental health, broadening the policy rationale.
insights.quote: the odds of being poor as you approach midlife are more than 10 times greater

insights.claim: Only 31% of lower-income Millennials are on track with the sequence versus 49% of middle-income and 65% of upper-income, and over half (52%) who skip or reorder steps are poor in their mid-30s.
insights.speaker: Alan J. Hawkins, Connie Huber
insights.so_what: Frames the sequence as most consequential precisely for the disadvantaged, the population policy aims to reach.
insights.locator: These findings are especially relevant for lower-income youth

insights.claim: Against the 'no real choice' critique (Philip Cohen), the authors side with Michael Tanner and Isabel Sawhill that denying the poor agency 'devalues them,' and cite practitioners like Thelma Moton's Choosing to Excel as proof the sequence can be taught to disadvantaged youth.
insights.speaker: Alan J. Hawkins, Connie Huber
insights.so_what: Maps the live ideological dispute over the sequence for readers tracking welfare-policy debates.
insights.quote: treating the poor with respect requires granting them agency
insights.locator: There is little doubt that structural barriers

insights.claim: The authors concede a real methodological limitation: the importance of sequencing needs confirmation from longitudinal (not just cross-sectional) studies, counseling 'some modesty' in claims pending more definitive research.
insights.speaker: Alan J. Hawkins, Connie Huber
insights.so_what: An honesty marker bounding the evidentiary strength of the core claim.
insights.quote: the importance of sequencing needs to be confirmed by longitudinal studies
insights.locator: Methodological critics of the success sequence

insights.claim: On the stigma critique, the authors agree with Isabel Sawhill that the sequence should be taught as an analytical 'well-worn path' to success rather than a strong normative framework of shoulds and should-nots, since young people resist normative framing.
insights.speaker: Alan J. Hawkins, Connie Huber
insights.so_what: A pedagogical positioning choice designed to make the curriculum politically durable across school boards.
insights.quote: as an analytical device to show a well-worn path to adult success, rather than as a strong normative framework
insights.locator: Here, we agree with Isabel Sawhill

insights.claim: Teaching the sequence has strong public support: more than three-quarters of American parents favor it in schools, including ~80% of Gen Z, 70% of Millennials, 74% of non-college Americans, 72% of Democrats, and 73% of those who didn't follow it themselves.
insights.speaker: Alan J. Hawkins, Connie Huber
insights.so_what: Bipartisan, cross-class support signals low political risk for states adopting the curriculum.
insights.quote: More than three-quarters of American parents favor teaching the success sequence in schools
insights.locator: Apparently not so much: the success sequence is overwhelmingly popular

insights.claim: The authors favor a multi-state federalist experiment over a top-down federal model, with states (often in better fiscal shape) leading and having 'skin in the game' rather than relying solely on the limited federal Administration for Children and Families funding since 2006.
insights.speaker: Alan J. Hawkins, Connie Huber
insights.so_what: Defines the governance model and funding locus for scaling the curriculum — relevant to where the policy action lands.
insights.quote: a multi-state, federalist experiment ultimately will be more effective than a top-down, one-size-fits-all model

insights.claim: Implementation should resolve the 'dosage dilemma' by integrating three to four drop-in modules (3-6 hours) into existing required and elective courses, since single lessons raise awareness but don't change behavior while full 8-10 hour curricula face content-displacement resistance.
insights.speaker: Alan J. Hawkins, Connie Huber
insights.so_what: A practical curriculum-design tradeoff that determines real-world adoption feasibility.

insights.claim: Funding can come from federal TANF block grants (explicitly designed for such educational purposes but seldom used so) and from setting aside a portion of marriage-license fees, overseen by a lean state commission or contracted private organization.
insights.speaker: Alan J. Hawkins, Connie Huber
insights.so_what: Identifies the concrete fiscal levers states would pull — a signal of which budget lines could see redirection.
insights.quote: Funding can come from federal TANF block grants
insights.locator: But they need to be funded appropriately

insights.claim: Ohio and Arkansas are already early movers, with Ohio's 'OhioMeansJobs Readiness Seal' and Arkansas's 'Student Success Plans' plus existing healthy-relationship education that the authors say could be linked into a synergistic success-sequence framework.
insights.speaker: Alan J. Hawkins, Connie Huber
insights.so_what: Names the leading states and existing programs (The Ridge Project, Choosing to Excel, Love Notes) to watch as adoption scales.
insights.locator: Several states, including Ohio and Arkansas

insights.claim: The authors engage Yuval Levin's critique of the sequence's 'coldness and thinness' — that a life is shaped by 'a proper layering of embedded commitments to others,' not a proper sequencing of solitary choices — and concede the point while recommending the sequence be taught as a means, not an end.
insights.speaker: Alan J. Hawkins, Connie Huber
insights.so_what: Shows the authors positioning the sequence as a roadmap-with-guardrails rather than a full theory of flourishing, hedging against conservative skepticism.
insights.quote: achievable not by a proper sequencing of solitary choices but by a proper layering of embedded commitments to others
insights.locator: Another friendly critic of the success sequence
